title: spring ins and outs
subtitle: it’s cold today, but in a spring way, and i love you
author: caitlynrichardson
raw_article:
hello.

spring is the season of in betweens. not quite the heaviness of winter or the fullness of summer. it’s a soft, slow unfolding of time where you can start opening windows again and notice how the rain makes everything smells a little cleaner. it’s about returning to yourself in a gentle way. like remembering what you like, what makes your days feel good, what actually feels nourishing when you’re not trying to force it.

every season i make little lists of ins and outs to see what i’m leaning into and what i’m leaving behind so i’m going to share that with you today.

a short sweet spring note.

also, if you haven’t read my full spring guide, here it is for your reference. you’ll find books, films, recipes, acitivites, music playlists and more.

(some of the things on this list are satire)

this post is free but consider becoming a paid subscriber to support this newsletter.

truly, your support means the world to me and it’s what gives me the capacity to write to you.

join my bookclub :)

philosophy books

classic literature

gothic literature

(follow tiktok, youtube, ig, pinterest and spotify for more)

milk fed is a reader-supported publication. To receive new posts and support my work, consider becoming a free or paid subscriber.

spring ins
-ren faire
i really just want to run around in a fairy costume and have someone feed me and watch beautiful people dance and prance around in cute outfits. i want to feel magical and buy marshmallow flavored honey and a sword and watch jousting matches and a get a leather satchel full of crystals and trinkets.

-planting your own herb garden
watching something grow that you actually take care of. it makes your space feel alive, and it’s small but grounding to use something you grew yourself.

-ambient fairy weather music
green-house and grouper and sounds that make you feel like you’re living inside of a tulip.

-hozier
he just belongs to this every season. earthy and feral and romantic. is he your forest daddy, too?

-biblical foods
simple, old, ritualistic. bread, honey, olives. figs, dried fruits, cured meats. cherry wine?

-chainmail armor
impractical and absolutely necessary. dressing like you’re part of another time just for the sake of it.

-gregorian chants by nuns
female voices only for this one. sing me to sleep. i fear this will be on my spotify wrapped this year.

-love divination performed on st. agnes’ eve
leaning into old traditions, even if you don’t fully believe in them. i want to feel a little mystical.

-wearing spring perfume
softer more melancholic perfumes that feel like a breath of cold morning air. something fresh, something floral, something ghostly, something strange. maybe you’re craving something witchy or something earthy and green.

-the use of mugwort for prophetic dreams
whether it works or not. i’m just creating space for reflection and paying attention to my inner world.

-medieval sleep charms and dream magic
taking sleep seriously, making it feel intentional. i want to feel cradled and safe. i want rituals that make rest feel protected and meaningful.

-muesli
shifting from warm porridge to cold oats, dried fruits, nuts, milk, brown sugar. there is nothing more refreshing.

-feasting (not fasting)
actually enjoying food, not restricting it. letting meals feel abundant, shared, and satisfying.

-april showers
not avoiding the rain, but leaning into it. letting it slow you down, make things more intimate and reflective.

-gothic spring
melancholic, overgrown ivy, stormy weather, ghostly victorian nightgowns, perfumes that smell like iris and wet stone and rain.

-game of thrones
watching for the first time and i’m completely entranced by this series. long, immersive worlds that you can disappear into. something that feels bigger than yourself.

-wearing nightgowns to the grocery store
a place to disassociate and feel comfortable in. make a hand written grocery list. buy fancy produce.

-lighting candles during the day because it’s storming
matching your environment instead of fighting it. letting your space reflect the mood outside. no more artificial light.

-keeping flowers slightly past the point of decay
not everything has to be perfectly fresh to be beautiful. letting flowers linger past their welcome.

-learning the names of wildflowers
paying attention to details you would normally ignore. it makes the world feel more specific and more real.

-freshly bread with butter and honey
baking from scratch. simple things done well. food that feels warm, comforting, and enough on its own.

-haunting your local cafe
becoming a regular somewhere. having a place that feels familiar, where you can just exist without the pressure to perform.

-collecting little objects (acorns, ribbons, pressed flowers)
holding onto small things that feel meaningful, even if no one else understands why. building your own little archive.

spring outs

-skipping breakfast
starting your day already depleted. it’s not worth it. eat something, even (and especially) if it’s simple.

-small talk
conversations that feel empty. if you’re going to talk, let it be something that actually matters. note: casual conversation is different than small talk.

-consuming more than creating
taking in constant input without giving anything back. even small acts of creating feel better.

-ignoring the weather
living the same way no matter what’s happening outside. the weather and the seasons should change how you move through your day.

-fast content / short attention spans
constantly scrolling without retaining anything. it leaves you feeling scattered and unsatisfied. if you’re going to immerse yourself in the internet world, make it worth your while. create a playlist of youtube episodes, podcasts, saved tiktoks and substack posts of things you want to return to when the feeling of doomscrolling calls your name.

-self help books
being told how to fix yourself over and over by pseudo experts. you’ll find more comfort, advice, therapy, solace in classic lit over self help, i promise.

-bad posture
i’m working on it.

-bras
unnecessary discomfort. if you don’t want to wear one, you don’t have to.

-the male gaze
the exhaustion of making choices based on how you’ll be perceived instead of how you actually feel.

-one sized friendship
relationships that don’t have room for you to change. the right ones adjust, grow, and make space and reciprocate energy back to you.

okay, that’s all i have for you today.

i love you. happy spring.

bye.

title: the most problematic maiden at the joust
subtitle: the medieval guide
author: caitlynrichardson
raw_article:
hello.

i want to create weekly newsletters series called the guide. basically, a way of building small, temporary worlds you can step into for a few days at a time. it will be made up of simple things like books, films, places to go, ways of spending an evening or an afternoon. the perfect way to escape the internet and dive deep into rabbit holes.

you might discover a new found interest or curiosity.

for this week’s guide, i’m focusing on the medieval.

what i find interesting about this world is the way life was structured. most people lived within a very narrow range, the same rooms, the same foods, the same routines, repeated daily. bread, porridge, preserved meat if you had it. clothing worn until it couldn’t be worn anymore. your day wasn’t something you optimized, it was something you moved through.

alongside that, you get the other layer of the medieval world. the one that’s lasted.

castles, not just as buildings but as as power. long tables, candlelight, fabric, ceremony. the idea of courtly love, loyalty, devotion, and all of it heightened, stylized, and turned into something otherworldly.

so you end up with both at once. a life that is materially limited, and a world that is symbolically excessive.

and while you obviously can’t recreate that, you can borrow from it in ways that resonate with you. so if you wanted to spend a few days inside is this world, this is where i’d start.

milk fed is a reader-supported publication. To receive new posts and support my work, consider becoming a free or paid subscriber.

books

these feel medieval in the way they hold you inside a world that has rules, hierarchy, and weight to it. you’re not skimming through them, you’re moving through something structured, where place, power, and routine actually matter.

the forgotten beasts of eld by patricia a. mckillip
this books makes life feel suspended in time. a woman alone on a mountain, surrounded by creatures that belong more to story than to reality, but the center of it isn’t really the magic, it’s what happens when isolation starts to harden into one’s identity, when being removed from the world begins to feel like safety, until something pulls you back into it.

the name of the rose by umberto eco
this is denser, slower, and more deliberate. it involves a monastery, a series of deaths, and a mind trying to make sense of it all. the setting and atmosphere of this book will keep you entranced. the feeling of stone and cold air, of knowledge being guarded and hidden. time moves in a way that feels almost sealed off from the outside world.

assassin’s apprentice by robin hobb
this one is more rooted in structure and hierarchy. it’s about what it means to grow up inside a system that has already decided who you are before you have any say in it. it follows a royal child raised on the edges of power, trained to become an assassin, and so much of it is about loyalty, identity, and learning how to exist within something that doesn’t fully make space for you. it’s very character driven, but it’s also deeply tied to the world it exists in, the rules, the expectations, the sense that everything has a place, whether you fit into it or not.

films / shows

these pull you into a world where everything costs something. love, loyalty, power. immersive world building and medieval romance at your disposal.

game of thrones
perhaps this is top of mine because i’m completely immersed in this world right now. the attention to how people actually live within the scale of this fantasy world is still something i’m trying to process. the coldness of certain places, the excess of others, the way power moves through rooms, conversations, bodies. everything feels structured around hierarchy, survival, allegiance, and even in its most extreme moments, there’s still a sense that this is a world with rules that people are constantly negotiating.

tristan & isolde
this is much more contained, and almost restrained in comparison. it’s a love story, but one that feels shaped entirely by obligation, loyalty, and the weight of a world that doesn’t allow for much deviation. there’s a heaviness to it in the way everything feels slightly held back, like emotion is always being managed, redirected, or denied.

activities to do

a medieval evening
choose one night and build it intentionally. turn off overhead lights before it gets dark and let the room fall into shadow, then use candles or a low lamp so parts of the room stay dim. make a simple meal: fresh bread, soft cheese, honey, roasted vegetables, maybe a fruit tart, with wine or spiced cider, and sit down to eat it slowly with no distractions.

a medieval girls night
have a small group over and make it into something slightly theatrical. create your own princess hennins using paper, fabric, or ribbon. nothing perfect, just handmade. watch tristan & isolde together, eat, talk, or sit in the atmosphere.

painting dragon eggs
do something physical and a little playful. paint decorative dragon eggs and make them look aged, metallic, or worn, like something you’d find hidden somewhere. it gives you something slow and tactile to focus on.

go to a ren faire
i’m going this year for the first time!! pay attention to clothing, textures, the way people move through the space. it’s one of the easiest ways to step into the atmosphere without having to create it yourself. i’m already dreaming of outfits i want to wear, snacks i want to try, and people i get to meet! if you live in new york or california, you should look into attending this year.

go to medieval times
lean into the theatrical version of this world. get dressed up, bring a group of friends. it’s exaggerated, but it gives you a sense of how performance, storytelling, and spectacle would have felt. if you’re in california, there are locations nearby; if you’re in new york, the new jersey one is a bit of a trek but worth it.

listen medieval music
make your own playlist or vibe out to mine :)

fragrance

comme des garcons avignon feels like stepping into a cathedral that’s been standing for centuries. it’s not crowded, or bright, but dim, vast, and cold. the kind of space where sound doesn’t travel the way you expect it to. the air is thick with incense, not fresh smoke, but something that’s settled into the walls over time, layered, resinous, almost heavy.

it opens immediately with that, frankincense and myrrh, deep and unmistakable, and then softens just slightly, something faintly herbal, a trace of warmth underneath.

serge lutens iris silver mist feels colder than you expect, not fresh or clean, but mineral, almost metallic, like something pulled up from the ground. the iris isn’t soft or powdery, it’s raw, sharp, damp, edged with something green and slightly bitter, like roots still holding onto earth.

it opens sharp, touched with spice, but never warm. everything feels restrained, held in place, and underneath it, woods and resin start to come through. it’s faintly smoky but still controlled.

you don’t have to do all of the things in this list, but even choosing one or two things is enough to change the feeling of your day.

that’s really the point of the guide. shifting your routine slightly and seeing what it feels like when your time is arranged a little differently.

for the next upcoming weeks, i already have a few themed in mind.

something centered around witches, something softer and greener like melancholic forest fairies, and something more gothic.

okay, that’s all for today. more guides coming soon…

if you have any specific topics you’d like me to discuss, comment them below!

i love you.

bye.

(follow tiktok, youtube, ig, pinterest and spotify for more)

title: big books that are actually worth your time
subtitle: it's time for your next project book
author: caitlynrichardson
raw_article:
hello.

one of the greatest literary experiences a reader can have is devouring a project book that changes your life.

these are the novels that are big and take up extra space on your shelf while you mentally prepare yourself to start them and extra space in your tote bag while you actually consume them.

and even though there are a handful of those huge classics that are not worth the effort, there are several that absolutely are and will potentially change your life if you take the time to read them. these are the kind of books that feel less like something you read and more like something you live inside for a while.

if you’ve been itching to start one, i have five recommendations for you...

the brothers karamazov

if there’s one book on this list that genuinely has the power to rearrange your inner life, it’s this one.

on the surface it’s about a deeply dysfunctional russian family and the murder of their father. the patriarch, fyodor karamazov, is crude, selfish, impulsive, and morally chaotic, and the lives of his sons orbit around the damage he leaves in his wake. when he is murdered, the question of who is responsible begins to unfold, but the novel quickly becomes something far larger than a simple crime story.

because the murder is really just the framework dostoevsky uses to explore much deeper questions about morality, faith, doubt, freedom, guilt, suffering, and what it actually means to be a human being.

each of the brothers represents a different way of moving through the world. dmitri is passionate, impulsive, ruled by emotion and desire. ivan is intellectual, skeptical, tormented by philosophical doubt and moral reasoning. alyosha is gentle, compassionate, deeply spiritual, someone who believes in love and forgiveness even in the face of human cruelty.

watching these three perspectives collide becomes a philosophical drama. the novel isn’t just interested in what happens to the characters, it’s interested in the ideas they represent and the questions they wrestle with.

again and again the book asks enormous moral questions that most people carry throughout their lives. if god does not exist, does that mean everything is permitted? how should we understand suffering, especially the suffering of innocent people? are human beings truly free, or are we driven by darker impulses we barely understand?

dostoevsky doesn’t approach these questions like a philosopher writing a neat argument. instead, he lets them emerge through the lives and struggles of the characters themselves. the ideas feel alive because they’re tied to real emotional stakes. love, jealousy, guilt, shame, longing for redemption.

there are moments in the novel that people remember for the rest of their lives.

ivan’s story of the grand inquisitor, for example, is one of the most famous passages in literature. in it, he imagines christ returning to earth during the spanish inquisition, only to be arrested by the church itself. the inquisitor argues that human beings don’t actually want freedom, they want security, certainty, and authority to tell them what to believe.

it’s a haunting story on religion, power, and the terrifying burden of human freedom.

but the novel isn’t only philosophical. it’s also deeply emotional and painfully human. the characters struggle with love, shame, resentment, guilt, and the longing to be forgiven for the worst things they’ve done.

dostoevsky is fascinated by the idea that human beings are rarely purely good or purely evil. instead, people contain contradictions. generosity alongside cruelty, compassion alongside selfishness, moments of grace alongside moments of terrible weakness.

what makes the novel so powerful is the way it insists on the reality of moral responsibility. even when people are shaped by suffering or circumstance, they are still accountable for the choices they make.

and yet, at the same time, the book is filled with an extraordinary sense of compassion. alyosha, in particular, represents the possibility that love, humility, and forgiveness might still exist even within a broken and chaotic world.

by the time you reach the end of the novel, it feels less like you’ve finished a story and more like you’ve spent time wrestling with the deepest questions about human life itself.

questions about justice, suffering, forgiveness.
about whether human beings are capable of redemption.

it’s one of those rare books that doesn’t simply entertain or even just move you emotionally.

it challenges and unsettles you.

and it forces you to think seriously about what kind of person you want to be and what it actually means to live a moral life in a complicated world.

anna karenina

tolstoy somehow manages to write a novel that feels like it contains the entire emotional landscape of human life.

on the surface, the story is simple. it begins with anna, a beautiful and intelligent woman trapped in a respectable but emotionally empty marriage, who falls passionately in love with another man. what follows is one of the most famous tragic love stories ever written, as anna slowly becomes consumed by a relationship that brings both ecstatic happiness and unbearable emotional turmoil.

but what makes the novel extraordinary is that anna’s story is only one part of it.

running alongside her life is the journey of levin, a landowner who is trying to figure out how to live meaningfully in a confusing world. while anna’s story is dramatic and destructive, levin’s is more introspective. he struggles with questions about work, family, love, faith, and the purpose of human life itself. he wrestles with doubt, with insecurity, with the strange feeling that even when life seems outwardly successful something essential might still be missing.

the novel moves between these two lives: anna’s descent into emotional chaos and levin’s slow, searching attempt to build a life that feels morally and spiritually grounded.

and what makes the book feel so incredible is the way tolstoy understands people. he seems to notice the smallest shifts in human emotion, the tiny moments where a relationship changes direction, the thoughts people have but rarely say out loud.

he captures the strange contradictions that exist inside nearly every human life. the way someone can be deeply loved and yet feel profoundly lonely. the way happiness can exist right alongside dissatisfaction. the way people often sabotage the very things they long for most.

again and again the novel reveals how much of human life is shaped by subtle inner forces. jealousy, insecurity, pride, longing, fear of judgment. tolstoy doesn’t present these emotions as exaggerated; instead, he shows how they operate beneath everyday life, slowly influencing the choices people make and the paths their lives take.

one of the most remarkable things about the novel is that none of the characters feel distant or symbolic. they feel startlingly real. their flaws are recognizable, their moments of happiness fragile, their mistakes painfully human.

as the story unfolds, you begin to realize that tolstoy isn’t just telling a tragic love story or a portrait of russian society. he’s trying to understand something much larger: what it means to live a good life.

through levin especially, the novel becomes a meditation on questions that almost everyone eventually faces. what makes life meaningful? how should we balance ambition, love, work, and morality? how do people find peace in a world that is often confusing and unstable?

by the end of the novel, there’s no neat answer to these questions. instead, there’s a sense that meaning isn’t found in these grand moments but in the smaller, often overlooked parts of life. family, honest work, kindness toward others, the willingness to live with humility and compassion.

and that’s why anna karenina feels so life changing.

by the time you finish it, you don’t just feel like you’ve read a story about anna and levin. it feels like you’ve been watching real human lives unfold with all their beauty, tragedy, and complexity.

and it leaves you thinking, long after you close the book, about how to actually live well.

east of eden

this is one of those rare novels that manages to feel both epic and deeply intimate at the same time. it stretches across generations, following two families living in california’s salinas valley, but somehow the story never loses its emotional closeness. the landscape, the passage of time, the slow unfolding of different lives. all of it gives the novel this mythical quality, and yet the emotional core of the book always remains deeply human and personal.

at the heart of the novel is one of the oldest questions in literature: are we destined to repeat the sins of the people who came before us, or do we actually have the freedom to choose who we become?

again and again, the characters seem to inherit patterns from the generations before them. rivalries repeat themselves between brothers. loneliness and resentment pass from parents to children. people find themselves shaped by wounds they didn’t choose and histories they didn’t create. steinbeck is deeply interested in how easily human beings fall into these inherited roles, how often we unconsciously reenact the same struggles that existed long before we were born.

and for much of the novel, there’s a haunting sense that these patterns might be inevitable. that people are trapped within the moral and emotional inheritance of their families.

but then the novel introduces one of its most important ideas, a single word that slowly transforms the entire meaning of the story: timshel.

steinbeck draws this idea from the biblical story of cain and abel. in the novel, a character studying the original hebrew text discovers that a key word in the passage has often been mistranslated. instead of meaning “thou shalt” or “thou must,” the word timshel means something much more than that.

it means “thou mayest.”

thou mayest overcome sin.
thou mayest choose a different path.
thou mayest become something other than what history expects of you.

this idea changes everything. suddenly the novel is no longer simply a story about inheritance or fate. it becomes a story about the terrifying freedom that comes with being human.

steinbeck begins to show how every character is standing at a moral crossroads, constantly faced with the possibility of choosing generosity instead of cruelty, forgiveness instead of resentment, love instead of bitterness. the characters are never perfectly good or perfectly evil; they exist in that deeply human space where people are capable of both tremendous kindness and tremendous harm.

what makes the novel so powerful is the compassion with which steinbeck approaches all of this. he doesn’t write about human weakness with judgment. instead, he seems endlessly fascinated by people. their flaws, longings, mistakes, their attempts to become better than they were yesterday.

by the time you reach the end of the novel, the idea of timshel begins to feel less like a literary theme and more like a philosophy for living. the book suggests that while we may inherit wounds, failures, and patterns from the past, none of those things completely determine who we are.

there is always the possibility of choosing something different.

and the idea that human beings are not permanently defined by their worst impulses, that redemption and transformation are always still possible, is what makes east of eden feel so deeply life changing.

by the time you close the book, it doesn’t just feel like you’ve finished a long novel. it feels like you’ve spent time thinking about what it actually means to be a human being, and about the strange, beautiful freedom we have to decide what kind of person we will become.

the count of monte cristo

this is a novel that is actually impossible to put down.

the story begins with a young sailor, edmond dantes, whose life is almost impossibly promising. he’s young, talented, deeply loved, and on the verge of becoming the captain of his own ship. he’s about to marry the woman he loves. everything in his life is just beginning to unfold.

and then, almost overnight, it’s all taken from him.

out of jealousy, ambition, and cowardice, the people around him conspire to falsely accuse him of treason. he’s arrested and thrown into a brutal prison where he disappears from the world for years, completely forgotten.

the early part of the novel is devastating because you watch someone who is essentially innocent slowly lose everything. his freedom, his youth, his identity, even his sense of hope.

but then something happens.

in prison, dantes meets an elderly scholar who becomes his teacher. through him he learns languages, science, philosophy, strategy. he essentially rebuilds himself intellectually and spiritually while the world believes he’s dead.

and when he finally escapes, he emerges not as the naive young man he once was, but as something entirely different.

he becomes the count of monte cristo.

what follows is one of the most intricate revenge plots ever written. dantes spends years patiently weaving together a series of elaborate schemes designed to destroy the people who betrayed him. he infiltrates their lives, studies their weaknesses, and slowly sets the stage for their downfall.

and on the surface, this part of the novel is just pure narrative pleasure. it’s dramatic, clever, suspenseful, and almost unbelievably satisfying to watch unfold.

but what makes the book so extraordinary is that it doesn’t stop there.

as dantes moves through his revenge, the novel begins asking more difficult questions. what happens to someone who dedicates their entire life to vengeance? what does it do to their soul?

because the deeper you go into the story, the clearer it becomes that revenge is not as clean or satisfying as it first appears. innocent people begin to get hurt along the way. the consequences of his schemes ripple outward in ways he didn’t fully anticipate.

and slowly, dantes begins to realize that the justice he imagined might not actually be justice at all.

the novel starts to explore the difference between justice and vengeance, between punishment and mercy, between power and forgiveness.

it asks whether a person who has suffered so deeply has the right to play the role of fate in other people’s lives.

by the end, the story that began as a revenge fantasy becomes something far more reflective and humane.

it becomes a meditation on suffering, forgiveness, patience, and the strange ways life unfolds beyond our control.

which is why the novel ends with one of the most beautiful lines in literature:

wait and hope.

and somehow, after a thousand pages of betrayal, imprisonment, revenge, and redemption, those two words feel like the entire point of the story.

middlemarch

george eliot once described it as “a study of provincial life,” which sounds modest, but the novel ends up becoming one of the most insightful stories of human psychology ever written.

it follows several characters whose ambitions, ideals, marriages, and careers slowly collide with reality. there are no grand wars or revolutions here, just the slow unfolding of ordinary lives, which somehow makes the novel feel even more real.

what eliot understands with incredible precision is the way people imagine their futures when they are young. the hopes they carry, the identities they build for themselves, the belief that life will unfold in a certain meaningful direction. and then, slowly, reality intervenes. marriages become more complicated than expected. ambitions falter. compromises appear where once there were ideals.

what eliot captures better than almost anyone is the subtle tragedy of ordinary life. the way people’s dreams shrink, shift, or evolve over time. but she also shows how meaning can still exist within those softer lives.

there’s a deep moral intelligence in the novel. eliot is endlessly curious about why people behave the way they do: how pride, self-deception, generosity, love, and insecurity shape the choices we make.

and what makes the book so powerful is that it never treats its characters harshly. even when they fail, even when they disappoint themselves, eliot approaches them with an extraordinary amount of empathy.

the novel ultimately suggests something very beautiful: that a good life doesn’t always look historically significant. sometimes the most meaningful lives are the quiet ones. the people who try, imperfectly, to be kind, to do some small good, to care for others.

and the book ends with one of the most moving ideas in literature: that the progress of the world often depends on the countless lives that history never records.

that even small acts of goodness ripple outward in ways we may never see.

and once you’ve read it, it becomes very difficult to think about your own life in quite the same way again.

if there’s one thing all of these books have in common, it’s this:

they’re long, yes. but they’re not just long for the sake of being long.

they’re the kind of novels that stay with you for years because they help you understand people, morality, love, and life itself just a little bit more clearly.

and that’s the kind of book that’s always worth the time.

one of the greatest literary experiences a reader can have is devouring aproject bookthat changes your life.
these are the novels that are big and take upextraspace on your shelf while you mentally prepare yourself to start them and extra space in your tote bag while you actually consume them.

and even though there are a handful of those huge classics that are not worth the effort, there are several that absolutely are and will potentially change your life if you take the time to read them. these are the kind of books that feel less like something you read and more like something youlive inside for a while.
if you’ve been itching to start one, i have five recommendations for you...
the brothers karamazov
if there’s one book on this list that genuinely has the power to rearrange your inner life, it’s this one.

on the surface it’s about a deeply dysfunctional russian family and the murder of their father. the patriarch, fyodor karamazov, is crude, selfish, impulsive, and morally chaotic, and the lives of his sons orbit around the damage he leaves in his wake. when he is murdered, the question of who is responsible begins to unfold, but the novel quickly becomes something far larger than a simple crime story.

because the murder is really just the framework dostoevsky uses to explore much deeper questions about morality, faith, doubt, freedom, guilt, suffering, and what it actually means to be a human being.

each of the brothers represents a different way of moving through the world. dmitri is passionate, impulsive, ruled by emotion and desire. ivan is intellectual, skeptical, tormented by philosophical doubt and moral reasoning. alyosha is gentle, compassionate, deeply spiritual, someone who believes in love and forgiveness even in the face of human cruelty.

watching these three perspectives collide becomes a philosophical drama. the novel isn’t just interested in what happens to the characters, it’s interested in the ideas they represent and the questions they wrestle with.

again and again the book asks enormous moral questions that most people carry throughout their lives. if god does not exist, does that mean everything is permitted? how should we understand suffering, especially the suffering of innocent people? are human beings truly free, or are we driven by darker impulses we barely understand?

dostoevsky doesn’t approach these questions like a philosopher writing a neat argument. instead, he lets them emerge through the lives and struggles of the characters themselves. the ideas feel alive because they’re tied to real emotional stakes. love, jealousy, guilt, shame, longing for redemption.
there are moments in the novel that people remember for the rest of their lives.

ivan’s story of the grand inquisitor, for example, is one of the most famous passages in literature. in it, he imagines christ returning to earth during the spanish inquisition, only to be arrested by the church itself. the inquisitor argues that human beings don’t actually want freedom, they want security, certainty, and authority to tell them what to believe.
it’s a haunting story on religion, power, and the terrifying burden of human freedom.

but the novel isn’t only philosophical. it’s also deeply emotional and painfully human. the characters struggle with love, shame, resentment, guilt, and the longing to be forgiven for the worst things they’ve done.

dostoevsky is fascinated by the idea that human beings are rarely purely good or purely evil. instead, people contain contradictions. generosity alongside cruelty, compassion alongside selfishness, moments of grace alongside moments of terrible weakness.

what makes the novel so powerful is the way it insists on the reality of moral responsibility. even when people are shaped by suffering or circumstance, they are still accountable for the choices they make.

and yet, at the same time, the book is filled with an extraordinary sense of compassion. alyosha, in particular, represents the possibility that love, humility, and forgiveness might still exist even within a broken and chaotic world.
by the time you reach the end of the novel, it feels less like you’ve finished a story and more like you’ve spent time wrestling with the deepest questions about human life itself.
questions about justice, suffering, forgiveness.about whether human beings are capable of redemption.
it’s one of those rare books that doesn’t simply entertain or even just move you emotionally.
it challenges and unsettles you.
and it forces you to think seriously about what kind of person you want to be and what it actually means to live a moral life in a complicated world.

title: art in translation
subtitle: the power of foreign films and translated literature
author: caitlynrichardson
raw_article:
hello.

most of us only ever encounter stories in the language we were raised in. we read books in it, watch films in it, move through the world assuming that the emotional vocabulary we grew up with is expansive enough to contain everything we might need. but it isn’t.

we live in a world with thousands of languages, thousands of ways to name a feeling, hold a memory, tell a story. and yet for most english speaking readers and viewers, our cultural world is mostly english. our literature is english. our cinema is english. the rhythms of speech, the pacing of conversations, the way characters express love or grief or anger all feel familiar because they belong to the same linguistic ecosystem we grew up inside.

translated literature and foreign films remind me how limited that world can be.

they remind me that language isn’t just about words. it’s about rhythm, silence, structure, even grammar. some languages hold entire emotional landscapes that english doesn’t. some cultures place emphasis in places that feel unfamiliar to me. sometimes a sentence arrives through translation feeling slightly strange or weighted in a way i can’t quite explain, and sometimes a film scene lingers on a moment longer than an american movie ever would. a look lasts too long. a silence stretches out. a conversation circles something rather than confronting it directly.

that can feel disorienting at first. but it can also be beautiful.

translation is not about creating perfect clarity. it’s about reaching across distance. it’s about approximation and trust. it’s about getting as close as possible to a feeling that was born somewhere else, in a different mouth, inside a different culture.

i think it’s easy to assume that translated literature is inaccessible or overly intellectual, but it’s actually the opposite. it asks us to slow down and listen. it reminds us that our way of thinking is not the default. it will inevitably make you a better reader, but it also makes you a better listener, a better observer, maybe even a better person.

i love the feeling of reading a sentence and knowing that, in its original language, it meant something slightly different. i love the tension in that. i love when a book feels like it has traveled across time and geography and somehow still found its way into my hands.

and i love when a translator leaves a note in the back explaining why they chose one word instead of another, how they tried to hold onto the shape of the original text while still making it sing in a new language. it reminds me that translation is an art form in itself. it’s a collaboration. it’s a bridge.

cinema does something similar.

watching a film made in another country often feels like reading in translation. the emotional language shifts. the pacing changes. people move through love, restraint, longing, and conflict in ways that feel unfamiliar. you start to realize that even the shape of a story is culturally specific.

that’s one of the reasons i love browsing through MUBI. their catalog is full of films from around the world that explore love in ways that feel more interior than the version we’re usually given.

here are a few that have stayed with me.

the hopeless romantic
 In the Mood for Love (2000)

two neighbors discover their spouses are having an affair with each other. instead of confronting them or immediately acting on their own attraction, they spend the film circling one another. meeting in hallways, sharing meals, rehearsing conversations they’ll never actually have. the romance exists almost entirely in what they don’t do, which is what makes it so devastating.

available in: Chile, Brazil, Argentina, Colombia, Mexico, India, Netherlands, Italy

the slow burn intellectual
 Portrait of a Lady on Fire (2019)

a painter is hired to secretly paint a woman who refuses to sit for a portrait. the two women spend days walking together, talking, observing each other, slowly becoming aware of what’s forming between them. the attraction builds through looking, listening, and shared time rather than overt action, until the inevitability of separation makes every moment feel charged.

available in: Argentina, Brazil, Chile, Colombia, Mexico, India, Netherlands, Germany, Canada, Australia

the philosophical sci-fi anime
 Ghost in the Shell (1995)

set in a near future cyberpunk world, the film follows a cyborg security officer investigating a hacker known as the puppet master. beneath the thriller plot is a philosophical exploration of identity, consciousness, and the boundaries between human and machine.

available in: Argentina, Brazil, Chile, Colombia, Mexico

the devastating romantic tragedy
 Decision to Leave (2022)

a detective investigating a mysterious death becomes increasingly entangled with the widow of the victim. their connection unfolds through suspicion, attraction, and emotional manipulation. it’s a love story told through surveillance, longing, and the strange intimacy of watching someone from a distance.

available in: India, Turkey, US, Canada, UK, Ireland, France, Netherlands, Australia

the obsessive / destructive lover
 The Piano Teacher (2001)

a repressed piano instructor living with her controlling mother enters a volatile relationship with one of her students. desire becomes tangled with humiliation, power, and emotional violence. it’s not a comforting romance. it’s a brutal examination of what happens when intimacy grows out of repression.

available in: Brazil, Argentina, Chile, Colombia, Mexico, Germany

the political memoir
Persepolis (2007)

based on marjane satrapi’s graphic memoir, the film follows her childhood in iran during the islamic revolution and her later life in europe. the stark black and white animation turns personal memory and political upheaval into something intimate and reflective. it’s both a coming of age story and a portrait of what it means to grow up between cultures.

available in: Germany, Australia

i love that MUBI makes room for all of these versions of love, not just the polished ones, but the complicated, interior, and difficult ones too. their catalog is full of films from around the world that explore love in ways that feel more interior than the version we’re usually given.

that same feeling of stepping into another emotional grammar is exactly why i love translated literature.

(poland)

territory of light by yūko tsushima

(japan)

the melancholy of resistance by lászló krasznahorkai
a mysterious circus arrives in a crumbling town, and the sense of doom spreads like fog. the sentences are long and winding, like someone trying to explain the end of the world without breathing. it’s about entropy, fear, and the terrifying ease with which people surrender to power when things start to fall apart. unsettling and prophetic in all the ways that matter. (hungary)

body.content: hello.

most of us only ever encounter stories in the language we were raised in. we read books in it, watch films in it, move through the world assuming that the emotional vocabulary we grew up with is expansive enough to contain everything we might need. but it isn’t.

we live in a world with thousands of languages, thousands of ways to name a feeling, hold a memory, tell a story. and yet for most english speaking readers and viewers, our cultural world is mostly english. our literature is english. our cinema is english. the rhythms of speech, the pacing of conversations, the way characters express love or grief or anger all feel familiar because they belong to the same linguistic ecosystem we grew up inside.
translated literature and foreign films remind me how limited that world can be.

they remind me that language isn’t just about words. it’s about rhythm, silence, structure, even grammar. some languages hold entire emotional landscapes that english doesn’t. some cultures place emphasis in places that feel unfamiliar to me. sometimes a sentence arrives through translation feeling slightly strange or weighted in a way i can’t quite explain, and sometimes a film scene lingers on a moment longer than an american movie ever would. a look lasts too long. a silence stretches out. a conversation circles something rather than confronting it directly.
that can feel disorienting at first. but it can also be beautiful.

translation is not about creating perfect clarity. it’s about reaching across distance. it’s about approximation and trust. it’s about getting as close as possible to a feeling that was born somewhere else, in a different mouth, inside a different culture.

i think it’s easy to assume that translated literature is inaccessible or overly intellectual, but it’s actually the opposite. it asks us to slow down and listen. it reminds us that our way of thinking is not the default. it will inevitably make you a better reader, but it also makes you a better listener, a better observer, maybe even a better person.

i love the feeling of reading a sentence and knowing that, in its original language, it meant something slightly different. i love the tension in that. i love when a book feels like it has traveled across time and geography and somehow still found its way into my hands.

and i love when a translator leaves a note in the back explaining why they chose one word instead of another, how they tried to hold onto the shape of the original text while still making it sing in a new language. it reminds me that translation is an art form in itself. it’s a collaboration. it’s a bridge.
cinema does something similar.

watching a film made in another country often feels like reading in translation. the emotional language shifts. the pacing changes. people move through love, restraint, longing, and conflict in ways that feel unfamiliar. you start to realize that even the shape of a story is culturally specific.
that’s one of the reasons i love browsing through MUBI. their catalog is full of films from around the world that explore love in ways that feel more interior than the version we’re usually given.
here are a few that have stayed with me.
the hopeless romanticIn the Mood for Love (2000)

two neighbors discover their spouses are having an affair with each other. instead of confronting them or immediately acting on their own attraction, they spend the film circling one another. meeting in hallways, sharing meals, rehearsing conversations they’ll never actually have. the romance exists almost entirely in what they don’t do, which is what makes it so devastating.
available in: Chile, Brazil, Argentina, Colombia, Mexico, India, Netherlands, Italy
the slow burn intellectualPortrait of a Lady on Fire (2019)

a painter is hired to secretly paint a woman who refuses to sit for a portrait. the two women spend days walking together, talking, observing each other, slowly becoming aware of what’s forming between them. the attraction builds through looking, listening, and shared time rather than overt action, until the inevitability of separation makes every moment feel charged.
available in: Argentina, Brazil, Chile, Colombia, Mexico, India, Netherlands, Germany, Canada, Australia
the philosophical sci-fi animeGhost in the Shell (1995)

set in a near future cyberpunk world, the film follows a cyborg security officer investigating a hacker known as the puppet master. beneath the thriller plot is a philosophical exploration of identity, consciousness, and the boundaries between human and machine.
available in: Argentina, Brazil, Chile, Colombia, Mexico
the devastating romantic tragedyDecision to Leave (2022)

a detective investigating a mysterious death becomes increasingly entangled with the widow of the victim. their connection unfolds through suspicion, attraction, and emotional manipulation. it’s a love story told through surveillance, longing, and the strange intimacy of watching someone from a distance.
available in: India, Turkey, US, Canada, UK, Ireland, France, Netherlands, Australia
the obsessive / destructive loverThe Piano Teacher (2001)

a repressed piano instructor living with her controlling mother enters a volatile relationship with one of her students. desire becomes tangled with humiliation, power, and emotional violence. it’s not a comforting romance. it’s a brutal examination of what happens when intimacy grows out of repression.
available in: Brazil, Argentina, Chile, Colombia, Mexico, Germany
the political memoirPersepolis (2007)

based on marjane satrapi’s graphic memoir, the film follows her childhood in iran during the islamic revolution and her later life in europe. the stark black and white animation turns personal memory and political upheaval into something intimate and reflective. it’s both a coming of age story and a portrait of what it means to grow up between cultures.
available in: Germany, Australia

i love that MUBI makes room for all of these versions of love, not just the polished ones, but the complicated, interior, and difficult ones too. their catalog is full of films from around the world that explore love in ways that feel more interior than the version we’re usually given.
